notes.anchor: Rupa's zeal in declaring games in your name
notes.note: Rupa appears to be an agent or freedman of Curio who proposed to announce public games (munera or ludi) in Curio's name while Curio was abroad. Cicero and Curio's other friends vetoed this as it would have pre-empted Curio's own choices on his return.

notes.anchor: anything should be done in your absence which, when you arrived, would no longer be open for you to decide
notes.note: A Roman magistrate or candidate who gave public games did so personally and at his own expense; declaring them in his name without his authorization would lock in obligations he had not chosen and rob him of the political credit.

notes.anchor: you can more easily attain everything that is most distinguished in the commonwealth by other means than by games
notes.note: Cicero's consistent advice is that earned oratorical and military reputation (virtus) outweighs the crowd-pleasing spectacle of expensive shows (munera); he thought Curio's political gifts strong enough that lavish games would be superfluous and financially ruinous.

notes.anchor: No man admires the capacity for these (it is a matter of means, not of merit)
notes.note: Roman public games were funded by the magistrate's personal fortune; Cicero's point is that the capacity to spend money signals wealth, not character or ability — a significant reservation given Curio's already precarious finances.
